flashcards.back: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection. It's the MAC protocol used in early Ethernet to handle shared media access.

flashcards.front: What is the minimum Ethernet frame size?
flashcards.back: 64 bytes. This ensures that a frame will occupy the link long enough for collision detection at maximum network segment length.

flashcards.front: What is the maximum Ethernet frame size (standard)?
flashcards.back: 1518 bytes (1500 bytes data + 18 bytes header/trailer). Frames larger than this are considered jumbo frames (if supported).

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the Preamble in an Ethernet frame?
flashcards.back: The 7-byte preamble provides clock synchronization between sender and receiver, allowing the receiving NIC to synchronize with the incoming data stream.

flashcards.front: What is a MAC address and how many bits is it?
flashcards.back: Media Access Control address is a unique 48-bit (6-byte) hardware address assigned to a network interface. Written as 12 hexadecimal digits (e.g., 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E).

flashcards.front: What is the function of the FCS field in Ethernet?
flashcards.back: Frame Check Sequence (4 bytes) contains a CRC checksum used to detect errors during transmission. If FCS doesn't match, the frame is discarded.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between 10BASE-T and 100BASE-TX?
flashcards.back: 10BASE-T supports 10 Mbps over Category 3 cables using 2 wire pairs; 100BASE-TX (Fast Ethernet) supports 100 Mbps over Category 5 cables using 2 wire pairs.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the SFD (Start Frame Delimiter)?
flashcards.back: SFD (10101011) is a 1-byte field that marks the end of the preamble and indicates the start of the actual frame data.
A process (defined in IEEE 802.3ab) where two connected devices automatically determine the highest common speed/duplex capability they both support.

flashcards.front: What is the maximum cable length for 10BASE-T?
flashcards.back: 100 meters (328 feet) between the host and the hub/switch, using twisted pair cabling.

flashcards.front: What does the EtherType field indicate in an Ethernet frame?
flashcards.back: A 16-bit field indicating which protocol is encapsulated in the data field (e.g., 0x0800 for IPv4, 0x0806 for ARP, 0x86DD for IPv6).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a hub and a switch in Ethernet?
flashcards.back: A hub operates at Physical layer (repeats signals to all ports); a switch operates at Data Link layer (learns MAC addresses and forwards frames only to intended port).

flashcards.front: What is half-duplex vs full-duplex Ethernet?
flashcards.back: Half-duplex allows only one-way communication at a time (uses CSMA/CD); full-duplex allows simultaneous sending and receiving (no collisions, no CSMA/CD needed).

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of VLAN tagging (802.1Q)?
flashcards.back: VLAN tagging adds a 4-byte tag to Ethernet frames to identify which VLAN a frame belongs to, enabling network segmentation and broadcast control.

flashcards.front: What is the Ethernet broadcast address?
flashcards.back: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (all 48 bits set to 1). Broadcast frames are delivered to all devices on the same network segment.
